fields.user_review: I wish I could give the first half of this book five stars. It took full advantage of the books epistolary format to create an inconsistent and underdetermined character sketch. The ever changing style and narrative made me feel like I was watching a chameleon made up of shades of grey taking every form it possible could, never letting me get too comfortable with my perception of the characters and their dynamics. Unfortunately, halfway through the book I felt myself getting more invested in Augustus and his friends. Any accounts by his enemies or competitors felt like they existed to explain what happened rather than to add nuance to the story. This made the second half feel like a normal (well written) story about a roman emperor that just happened to be told through letters and journal entries. That being said I still really enjoyed it and the ending got me pretty emotional.
Not my cup of tea. Found it boring.
I don’t think I get it :(
Tempted to catapult myself into space to find a sixth star for this book

Maturing is realising that you don’t need to know whether the authors narrative mirrored Moctezuma’s character or if Moctezuma was simply a reflection of the unpredictable narrative. Much like the emperor himself, this book doesn’t take itself too seriously. This fact makes glimpses into the author’s deep knowledge of and admiration for this period in Mesoamerican history so exciting. Similarly, the experience of the emperor’s whimsy being fragmented by his strategic expertise and cruelty feels like a protest against stereotypical and often imported ideas of authority and respect.
Amazing book! Really enjoyed the writing style.

I wish I could give the first half of this book five stars. It took full advantage of the books epistolary format to create an inconsistent and underdetermined character sketch. The ever changing style and narrative made me feel like I was watching a chameleon made up of shades of grey taking every form it possible could, never letting me get too comfortable with my perception of the characters and their dynamics. Unfortunately, halfway through the book I felt myself getting more invested in Augustus and his friends. Any accounts by his enemies or competitors felt like they existed to explain what happened rather than to add nuance to the story. This made the second half feel like a normal (well written) story about a roman emperor that just happened to be told through letters and journal entries. That being said I still really enjoyed it and the ending got me pretty emotional.
